coredata.dc:title: Cancer intra and extracellular microenvironment-responsive electrical signal of touchable conductive hydrogel
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jo, Hayeon
Ryplida, Benny
Roy, Kaustuv
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: A wireless-integrated tumor microenvironment-responsive sensor designed from diselenide-functionalized carbon dot (dsCD) and hyaluronic acid polymer dot (PD(HA)) loaded in a mineralized conductive hydrogel (dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel) for detecting cellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) and pH detection. The manufactured ROS/pH responsive conductive hydrogel efficiently differentiated between cancer cells and normal cells in response to cellular factors through changes in fluorescence, electrical and mechanical response. Tumor microenvironment acidity and high ROS concentrations caused fluorescence recovery via boronate ester and diselenide bond cleavage alongside a change in electrical resistivity from 78.9 kΩ (pH 7.4) to 38.8 kΩ (0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8) due to disintegration of molecular interaction in the sensor. In vitro electrochemical analysis revealed different ΔR/R0 bending profiles between cancer (26.2 % and 25.9 % for HeLa and PC-3, respectively) and normal cells (17.9 %), alongside the optical performance demonstrating higher regain of fluorescence of cancer cells (HeLa and PC-3) compared to the normal cells (CHO-K1). Additionally, the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel demonstrated favorable biocompatibility and in vitro ROS scavenging, with selective targeting of cancer cells. A wireless sensing platform was developed by connecting the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel sensor to a smartphone, showing a precise signal during detection via resistance or electro-mechanical signals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ROS/pH-dual responsive
Fluorescence recovery
Touchable electrochemical sensor
ROS scavenging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of boronic acid-modified hyaluronic acid polymer dots [PD(HA)] and dsCD-crosslinked PD(HA) [dsCD-PD(HA)]
Fabrication of dsCD-PD(HA) embedded dual-responsive conductive hydrogel [dsCD-PD(HA) hydrogel]
Optical fluorescence, swelling ratio and mechanical properties of dsCD-PD(HA)-Hydrogel
Electrical resistance, motion sensing, wireless-connected motion analysis of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel
In vitro stable- and selective- electrical resistivity of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel
In vitro investigation of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel
Preparation and characterization of H2O2- and pH-sensitive dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles
Fabrication and properties of Dual-Sensitive dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel
The H2O2- and pH-responsive electrical signal of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel by mechanical deformation
In vitro study of cancer cell selective electrical signal using dsCD-PD(HA) hydrogel sensor

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of H2O2- and pH-sensitive dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles, (b) UV–vis spectra, (c) hydrodynamic diameter, and (d) zeta potential of dsCD-PD(HA) at various treatment condition (pH 7.4, pH 6.8, 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 7.4, and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8) for n = 3 samples; unpaired student’s t test was used for statistical analysis (*P < 0.05; **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001), (e) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of dsCD-PD(HA) treated at pH 7.4 (left) and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8 (right), (f) Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of dsCD-PD(HA) treated at pH 7.4 (top) and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8 (bottom).

(a) Schematic of h2O2– and pH-responsive dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel, (b) confocal images and (c) SEM images of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel at various treatment conditions (pH 7.4, pH 6.8, 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 7.4, and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8). (d) Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and (e) Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel were conducted under treatment conditions of 0 mM H2O2/pH 7.4 and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8.

(a) Stress–strain curve of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel after exposure to compression force, (b) compressive toughness and compressive modulus of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel for n = 3 samples, and (c) measured sourcemeter resistance for n = 3 samples; unpaired student’s t test was used for statistical analysis (*P < 0.05; **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001), ns: no statistical significance. (d) Nyquist plot, and (e) wireless analysis data of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel under varied treatment conditions (pH 7.4, pH 6.8, 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 7.4, and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8).

(a) Visual images of dscd-pd(ha) hydrogel during various bending scenarios. (b) Relative resistance data of dscd-pd(ha) hydrogel after being deformed by bending 2. (c) Relative resistance data of the dscd-pd(ha) hydrogel correlated with the degree of bending (0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, and 120°). (d) Wireless analysis data of dscd-pd(ha) hydrogel after being bent at 90°.

(a) Measured sourcemeter resistance for n = 3 samples, unpaired student’s t test was used for statistical analysis (*P < 0.05; **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001), and (b) wireless analysis data of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel-treated with CHO-K1, HeLa, and PC-3 cells. (c) The relative resistance result of the cells-treated dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel when bent at different angle (0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, and 120°).

(a) Live and dead cell viability assay, (b) cellular uptake images of cho-k1,HeLa, and PC-3-embedded dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel, (c) ROS scavenging capacity of hydrogel observed in CHO-K1, HeLa, and PC-3 cells using dihydroethidium (DHE) dye.

head.ce:title: Cancer intra and extracellular microenvironment-responsive electrical signal of touchable conductive hydrogel

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of IT and Energy Convergence (BK21 FOUR), Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 27469, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Industry Institute, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 27469, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 27469, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of IT and Energy Convergence (BK21 FOUR), Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 27469, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of IT and Energy Convergence (BK21 FOUR)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A wireless-integrated tumor microenvironment-responsive sensor designed from diselenide-functionalized carbon dot (dsCD) and hyaluronic acid polymer dot (PD(HA)) loaded in a mineralized conductive hydrogel (dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel) for detecting cellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) and pH detection. The manufactured ROS/pH responsive conductive hydrogel efficiently differentiated between cancer cells and normal cells in response to cellular factors through changes in fluorescence, electrical and mechanical response. Tumor microenvironment acidity and high ROS concentrations caused fluorescence recovery via boronate ester and diselenide bond cleavage alongside a change in electrical resistivity from 78.9 kΩ (pH 7.4) to 38.8 kΩ (0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8) due to disintegration of molecular interaction in the sensor. In vitro electrochemical analysis revealed different ΔR/R0 bending profiles between cancer (26.2 % and 25.9 % for HeLa and PC-3, respectively) and normal cells (17.9 %), alongside the optical performance demonstrating higher regain of fluorescence of cancer cells (HeLa and PC-3) compared to the normal cells (CHO-K1). Additionally, the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel demonstrated favorable biocompatibility and in vitro ROS scavenging, with selective targeting of cancer cells. A wireless sensing platform was developed by connecting the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel sensor to a smartphone, showing a precise signal during detection via resistance or electro-mechanical signals.

ce:section.ce:para: Known as one of the deadliest diseases in the world, cancer has become a threat that can no longer be overlooked [1]. The main cause of cancer has been identified as the changes or mutations that occur in cells, particularly in the genes that are made up of DNA, leading to malfunction of genes and uncontrolled growth and division of cells [2]. This mutation and proliferation would alter cell growth, as well as the concentration of chemical compounds present within the cells [3,4]. Within a decade, tumor extracellular pH was found to be more acidic (pH 6.4–6.8) than that of normal cells (pH 7.2–7.4), and is frequently referred to as physiological pH [5–7]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as H2O2, are significantly elevated in tumor cells. Under normal conditions, the physiological concentration of ROS regulates the cell cycle and apoptosis, which are important factors for cell growth [8,9]. However, ROS levels are known to be higher in cancer cells than in normal cells, which leads to the destruction of cells and oxidative stress at the tumor site [10]. Therefore, identifying the tumor site and reducing peroxide concentration are important for the detection and chemo-recovery of the disease.

Emerging materials such as hydrogel have been explored for biosensing applications, particularly for the detection of cancer [11,12]. The most well-known application of these techniques is monitoring of changes in the physicochemical characteristics of hydrogels, such as swelling behavior in response to biological interactions, and thereby detecting biological event using hydrogels immobilized with bioreceptors [13]. This use also depends on the physical and chemical interactions between the targeted biomolecules in the physiological surroundings and bioreceptors [14]. For instance, particular physiological conditions can be used to detect changes in the swelling characteristics of different hydrogels, such as ionic-, pH-, and temperature-sensitive hydrogels [15]. Additionally, hydrogel receptors can bind to specific biological molecules, such as proteins, genes, and other biomolecules, allowing for the identification of diseases by observing biochemical responses via optical and/or electrochemical analyses [16,17]. Although the majority of hydrogel-based biosensors have been shown to be very sensitive and selective for the detection of target molecules, the analysis is still overly time-consuming, costly, and complex to be employed in a clinical environment [18]. Establishing a direct and reliable detection method is essential, enabling its use by both non-experts in biosensing disciplines and healthcare professionals.

In this study, we fabricated a ROS/pH responsive conductive hydrogel (dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel) based on the loading of carbonized diselenide-functionalized polydopamine (dsCD) crosslinked with boronic acid-conjugated hyaluronic acid polymer dot (PD(HA)) through diol-diol coordination, which was used as pH-responsive and H2O2-sensitive moieties. The sensor was incorporated with dual responsive dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles into a polyacrylic acid (PAA) matrix and then mineralized by the in situ formation of amorphous calcium carbonate (ACC) which provided ionic conductivity and viscoelastic flexibility alongside a facile fabrication process. The fabricated hydrogel exhibited remarkable capability in distinguishing cancer cells from normal cells in response to cellular factors. This was evidenced by the higher fluorescence intensity of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel at acidic pH and higher ROS concentration. The acidic microenvironment in tumor cells triggered the recovery of fluorescence of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel via cleavage of the boronate ester bond in dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles, which were initially quenched by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect. On the other hand, a higher concentration of H2O2 in the cells impacts the mechanical, optical, and electrical performances of the hydrogel by cleaving the diselenide bond. Furthermore, the presence of catechol groups on dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel endowed the sensor with adhesiveness allowing for bending motion analysis. Considering the sensitivity of electrochemical biosensor to cellular pH and H2O2 concentration, developing a reliable smartphone-connected sensing system can enable precise monitoring and detection of cancer cells.

Hyaluronic acid (HA, Research Grade, HA 15M) was purchased from Lifecore Biomedical, South Korea. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), 4-bromobutyryl chloride, 3-aminophenylboronic acid monohydrate, polyacrylic acid (PAA), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), selenium (Se), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethyl carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), triethylamine (TEA), dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), trizma-base, phosphate buffer saline (PBS), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (Seoul, South Korea). Laponite was bought from TALAS (Brooklyn, USA). Calcein-acetoxymethyl (Calcein-AM) dye and propidium iodide (PI) dye were purchased from Invitrogen, Life Technologies (Carlsbad, USA). Penicillin–streptomycin, phosphate‐buffered saline (PBS), 0.05 % (w/v) trypsin-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (trypsin- EDTA) 1 × solution, Minimum Essential Medium (MEM), Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 Medium, and Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) were procured from Gibco BRL (Carlsbad, USA), Triton™ X-100, Trizma hydrochloride (Tris-HCl), Sodium Chloride (NaCl) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (Seoul, South Korea) and OxiTec™ Hydrogen Peroxide / Peroxidase Assay Kit were procured from BIOMAX (Gyeonggi-do, South Korea). The carbonized diselenide-functionalized polydopamine (dsCD) and the carbon/polymer dots used in this study were prepared via hydrothermal method was prepared as by previously reported procedure [19–21].

A UV–visible Spectrophotometer was used to analyze the absorbance spectra (Ubi-490, MicroDigital, South Korea). A nanoPartica SZ-100BV2 (Horiba scientific, Japan) was used to examine the zeta potentials and the particle size. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected via a Scinco FluoroMate fluorometer (South Korea). Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with Nicolet iS10 series (USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded with a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope (Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were documented through a PSIA XE-100 atomic force microscope (South Korea). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained using a JEOL JSM-6700F (Japan). Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) images were captured using a Nikon ECLIPSE Ti2-E confocal microscope (Japan). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and thermal gravimetry analysis (TGA) were investigated with a DSC2910 TA Instrument (USA). Stress–strain tensile and compression graph was recorded using a SurTA 1Ae texture analyzer (South Korea). The rheological strain sweep was analyzed with a HAAKE MARS (South Korea) with a 40 mm parallel plate geometry. Resistance and pressure sensing signals were estimated using a Keithley 2450 Sourcemeter (USA). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data was recorded using a Corrtest Potentiostat CS350 series (China). A self-made wireless device consisting of an Arduino Uno microcontroller (ATmega328P Processor), a smartphone, a self-made analogous circuit, and Bluetooth modules (AppGosu) was used to assess the real-time sensing capacity of the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of boronic acid-modified hyaluronic acid polymer dots [PD(HA)] and dsCD-crosslinked PD(HA) [dsCD-PD(HA)]
ce:section.ce:para: To prepared dsCD-crosslinked PD(HA), each of the separate nanoparticle, dsCD and PD(HA) was prepared as followed [16]. The boronic acid conjugated HA was initially prepared by attaching the 3-aminophenylboronic acid monohydrate into the hyaluronic acid polymer via EDC/NHS coupling reaction. An amount of HA (1 g) was dissolved completely in a 2 × PBS (pH 3.0), followed by addition of EDC (0.30 g) and NHS (0.18 g). Then, 0.16 g of 3-aminophenylboronic acid monohydrate was added into the solution and stirred for 24 h in room temperature. The mixture was then dialysis against water (Mw = 3.5 kDa) and lyophilized. The obtained solid was then dissolved again in water, placed in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h and freeze-dried. The dsCD-crosslinked PD(HA) was prepared by dissolving dsCD and PD(HA) with a mass ratio of 1:10 in TBS pH 8.5 for 24 h at room temperature. The mixed solution was dialyzed against water (Mw = 1 kDa) overnight and freeze-dried to obtain powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of dsCD-PD(HA) embedded dual-responsive conductive hydrogel [dsCD-PD(HA) hydrogel]
ce:section.ce:para: Dual pH- and ROS-responsive dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was prepared according to the following procedure: briefly, laponite (140 mg) was dispersed in 2 mL polyacrylic acid solution for 1 h at room temperature while stirring. Then, 14 mg dsCD-PD(HA) was added into the mixture and continuously stirred for 30 min. This was followed by the careful addition of 15 mL of 2 M CaCl2 solution and allowed to mix for 5 min. Thereafter, 3 mL of 1 M Na2CO3 was added in a dropwise manner to complete the mineralization procedure via CaCO3 formation [22,23]. The obtained hydrogel was then thoroughly washed with distilled water to remove the excess CaCl2 and Na2CO3 and molded on a small petri dish to provide proper shape. The formed dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was then stored in a 4 °C before further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical fluorescence, swelling ratio and mechanical properties of dsCD-PD(HA)-Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The optical fluorescence of the hydrogel was observed through confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The measurement was conducted to the hydrogel with the approximate mass of 30 mg. The hydrogel then placed on the SPL confocal and observed using ECLIPSE Ti2-E confocal microscope (Nikon, Japan) with 408 nm (DAPI) and 488 nm (EGFP) laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical resistance, motion sensing, wireless-connected motion analysis of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The conductivity of the samples was analyzed using EIS and measured with a Corrtest instrument using a two-electrode system. The frequency was set to 0.1 Hz–10 kHz and the measurement was performed at 0 V vs the OCP. The electrical resistivity and the motion sensing response during bending (0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, and 120°) were recorded with a Keithley 2450 Sourcemeter at room temperature with a BIAS voltage of 1 V. Electronic wireless analysis was conducted by connecting self-prepared wireless devices to the hydrogel and transmitting the result to the smartphone via a Bluetooth connection [21]. The prepared system consists of a self-made electronic circuit, a microcontroller (Arduino Uno), and a Bluetooth module (AppGosu). The performance was displayed as a pattern of active communication between the smartphone and the measurement system. The performance of the bending signals for 50 cycles was conducted by using the treated dsCD-PD(HA) hydrogel at pH 7.4, 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8. The resistance of the hydrogel in various stimuli such as glucose, ascorbic acid, were investigated at 10 mM concentration of solution. For horse serum, fetal bovine serum (FBS), horse serum a 1 % solution was used. pH was adjusted for each solution at 7.4 and 6.8. For the mix solution of interfering agent at various pH, they were mixed with 0.1 mM H2O2 at a ratio of 1:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro stable- and selective- electrical resistivity of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro electrochemical performance of the dsCD-PD(HA) hydrogel was analyzed by treating the hydrogel with CHO-K1 as the normal cells and HeLa, PC-3 as the cancer cells. To evaluate the selective-electrical resistivity of dsCD-PD(HA) hydrogel treated with each solution, the dsCD-PD(HA) hydrogel was treated with (1) Normal–simulated human serum was prepared from stock by making 1 % solution, whereas, cancer-simulated human serum was prepared by adding 0.1 mM H2O2 to 1 % solution and adjusting the pH to 6.8 and treated with 1 g of the hydrogel of dimension 10 × 10 × 10 mm (for n = 3 samples); (2) cell-grown media (for 0 day and 3 days); (3) Cell lysis was performed using the prepared 1 % Triton X-100 buffer solution. After cell culture, the obtained cell pellet was treated with a 1 % Triton X-100 buffer solution for 30 min and sonication (pulse mode, 5 min) was performed. Then, centrifugation was carried out at 14,000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained supernatant had a concentration of 106 cells/100 µL which was diluted to obtain the cell lysis solution; and 4) a mixed solution of the cell-grown media and the diluted cell lysis solution was used to observe the dual effect of real samples. Each type of treatment solution (2–4) was obtained from CHO-K1 as the normal cells and HeLa, PC-3 as the cancer cells which was further treated with 0.5 g of the hydrogel of dimension 4 × 10 × 10 mm (for n = 3 samples). Additionally, For the H2O2 assays of cells, the protocol of BIOMAX OxiTec™ hydrogen peroxide/peroxidase assay kit was followed [24]. For the analysis of the intracellular content of H2O2, H2O2 solution at the concentration determined from H2O2 assays of cells were prepared followed by treatment with 0.5 g of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel of dimension 4 × 10 × 10 mm (for n = 3 samples). The electrical resistivity for dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel at all treatment solutions (1–4) and with H2O2 solution at different concentration after H2O2 assays of cells were recorded with a SourceMeter (Keithley 2450) at room temperature with a BIAS voltage of 1 V. The in vitro bending motion response of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was conducted with the same parameters as in the ex vitro experiment for n = 3 samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro investigation of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Normal cells (CHO-K1) and cancer cells (HeLa and PC-3) were cultured at a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2, 37 °C. After incubation, the stock cells solution was dropped into the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel that was previously placed in the 24-wells culture plate. The cells concentration for each well was set at 1 × 105 cells/mL and the cells-embedded dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was incubated for different treatment time (12, 24, and 48 h). The cellular uptake was measured using confocal laser scanning microscope with 488 nm (EGFP) laser. The confocal images of live and dead assay were measured after adding the Calcein-AM dye and PI dye. For investigating the ROS level of cells, dihydroethidium (DHE) was used to stained the CHO-K1, HeLa, and PC-3 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of H2O2- and pH-sensitive dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
The dual pH- and H2O2-responsive nanoparticles were synthesized from carbonized diselenide-functionalized polydopamine (dsCD) and boronic acid-hyaluronic acid polymer dots [PD(HA)], as illustrated in Fig. S1. The nanoparticles were then conjugated by boronate ester formation between the catechol functional group of dsCD and the boronic acid of PD(HA) [25]. After crosslinking, the fluorescence intensity of dsCD-PD(HA) was reduced, which was controlled by the FRET effect of the crosslinked polydopamine and the introduction of diselenide into dsCD [26,27]. Furthermore, the aggregation behavior of the nanoparticles could potentially result in π-π interactions between dsCD and PD(HA), consequently reducing the fluorescence intensity of dsCD-PD(HA) [28,29]. In an acidic environment and at a high ROS level, the boronate ester bonds disintegrate, and the diselenide bonds were oxidized to form –Se–OOH, recovering the optical fluorescence and electrical conductivity (Fig. 1
a) [29,30].

The UV–vis spectra of dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles in response to external stimuli, such as pH and H2O2, were examined at a concentration of 0.01 mg/mL. The treatment pH was set at 7.4 and 6.8, which mimic the physiological pH of normal cells and the extracellular pH of the tumor environment, respectively [31]. Moreover, nanoparticles were treated with 0.1 mM H2O2 based on the intracellular H2O2 concentration of cancer cells [32,33]. The absorbance peak at 285.8 nm was assigned to the π-π* transition of the catechol group of dsCD nanoparticles (Fig. S2a) [34]. Additionally, the presence of boronic acid was confirmed by peaks at 240.7 nm and 320.5 nm, which are correlated to the absorption band of phenylboronic acid in PD(HA) [35]. Following the formation of dsCD-PD(HA), the disappearance of boronic acid peaks were observed, indicating successful interaction between the catechol and the boronic acid functional groups of each species [36]. Treatment at pH 6.8, either without or with H2O2, resulted in the emergence of peak at 240.2 nm, which is correlated with the boronic acid peak of PD(HA), indicating disintegration of boronate ester bonds ( Fig. 1 b). Moreover, treatment with 0.1 mM H2O2 led to an augmentation in the overall spectrum and a shift in the absorption peaks, corelating with an upsurge of solubility of dsCD-PD(HA) owing to the cleavage of the diselenide bonds of dsCD and alterations in nanoparticle size [37,38].

The hydrodynamic diameter of dsCD-PD(HA) in different external solutions was measured. Initially, the particle sizes of PD(HA) and dsCD were measured (Fig. S2b), showing an average diameter of 27.2 nm and 218.7 nm, respectively, which increased to 490.0 nm after the formation of dsCD-PD(HA). The change in particle size was observed with a change in the pH condition: a peak of 490.0 nm at pH 7.4 and two peaks of 36.5 nm and 225.8 nm at pH 6.8. The two separate peaks at pH 6.8 represented the cleavage of the diol-diol group of boronate ester bonds, splitting dsCD and PD(HA). Furthermore, the introduction of 0.1 mM H2O2 into the external solution resulted in a size reduction to 255.9 nm (0.1 mM H2O2/pH 7.4) and the appearance of two peaks at 30.9 nm and 127.3 nm (0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8), indicative of diselenide bond disintegration (Fig. 1 c). The zeta potential of dsCD-PD(HA) at different treatments was evaluated to further study the surface phenomena, as displayed in Fig. 1 d. The surface charge of pH-treated dsCD-PD(HA) demonstrated a zeta potential of −13.5 and −20.0 mV at pH 7.4 and pH 6.8, respectively. Additionally, in the presence of 0.1 mM H2O2, the zeta potential shifted to −17.5 and −26.8 mV at pH 7.4 and pH 6.8, respectively. The shift in the surface charge indicated the presence of various functional groups on the surface of dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained to clearly show the size reduction of nanoparticles due to environmental pH and the surrounding H2O2. As shown in Fig. 1 e, the diameter of dsCD-PD(HA) was smaller at 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8 treatment compared to that at pH 7.4, which agrees with the DLS result. Furthermore, as depicted in the highlighted inset image, dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles at pH 7.4 and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8 displayed a constant lattice spacing of approximately 0.26 and 0.24, which is indicative of the presence of graphitic core structure in the nanoparticles in normal and cancer microenvironment. Moreover, atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles on a silicon wafer substrate revealed a decrease in surface roughness following exposure to pH 6.8 and H2O2, providing additional evidence for the reduction in nanoparticle size resulting from the cleavage of boronate ester and diselenide bonds of dsCD-PD(HA) post-treatment (Fig. 1 f). Additionally, the dual effect of cancer environmental pH and H2O2 on the fluorescence of the nanoparticles was evaluated using PL spectroscopy as given in Fig. S3. The dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles at pH 7.4 demonstrated decreased FL intensity owing to the presence of boronate ester bonds as compared to pH 6.8. Moreover, the introduction of 0.1 mM H2O2 demonstrated an additional increase in the FL intensity caused by the dissolution of diselenide bonds demonstrating the FL on/off phenomenon. Furthermore, FT-IR spectra (Fig. S4) of dsCD-PD(HA) at pH 7.4 showed the presence of boronate ester bonds at 1130 cm−1 and 880 cm−1 associated with the B-O infrared absorption and C–O stretching vibration [39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and properties of Dual-Sensitive dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The H2O2– and pH-responsive conductive hydrogels were prepared by incorporating dsCD-PD(HA) into the polyacrylic acid (PAA) matrix in the presence of laponite and Ca2+ ions from CaCl2 and Na2CO3, utilized for cancer detection (Fig. S5). Owing to the contrasting pH and ROS levels between physiological conditions and the tumor environment, the mechanical and electrical performance of the hydrogel is influenced, facilitating disease detection. Under normal conditions, the cross-linking promoted by the ionic complex of Ca2+ and laponite with carboxylic functional groups was retained. However, variations in pH and H2O2 concentration presented by cancer cells altered the molecular state, cleaving the boronate ester bonds and diselenide bonds, revealing the H2O2– and pH-responsiveness of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel.

The physical properties of the hydrogel were initially assessed by investigating its water absorption and retention performances. The water retention capacity of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was calculated, as shown in Fig. S6. At various treatment conditions, the swelling ratio of the hydrogel was measured as 48.1 %, 49.1 %, 53.0 %, and 55.8 % at pH 7.4, pH 6.8, 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 7.4, and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8, respectively. The increase in the swelling ratio post-H2O2 treatment was influenced by the presence of Se-OOH, which enhanced the hydrophilicity and water absorption of the hydrogel. Furthermore, confocal images of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogels were evaluated to study the optical fluorescence under different treatment conditions, as shown in Fig. 2
b. At pH 7.4, the fluorescence was quenched due to the FRET phenomenon of the polydopamine (PDA) of dsCD, reducing the fluorescence intensity after the formation of boronate ester bonds between the two nanoparticles. The fluorescence recovered at pH 6.8 (bright green), which involved the disintegration of the pH-sensitive boronate ester bonds.

Additionally, the impact of pH and H2O2 on the morphology of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was visualized using SEM (Fig. 2 c), uncovering an enlarged pore size of the conductive hydrogel in response to external stimuli. It can be assumed that the mechanical properties of the hydrogel were affected by the change in morphology. The rheological strain sweeps of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel showed an upsurge in storage modulus (G’) from 4.1 kPa (pH 7.4) to 6.5 kPa after the adjustment of pH to 6.8 and H2O2 concentration (0.1 mM) at the shear strain of 1 % (Fig. S7). Furthermore, the loss modulus (G”) showed a similar pattern as that of G’, with G” values of 7.5 kPa and 11.1 kPa at pH 7.4 and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8, respectively. The presence of Ca2+, laponite, and dsCD-PD(HA) influenced the mechanical properties of the hydrogel by enhancing the tensile and compression mechanics of the mineralized PAA hydrogel [41–43]. Moreover, the increase in the modulus of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was influenced by a reorganization in the intermolecular network post-treatment with acidic pH and H2O2. This process involved the formation of Se-OOH following the oxidation of Se–Se by H2O2. To investigate this phenomenon, we conducted differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) at pH 7.4 and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8. As depicted in Fig. 2 d, the glass transition temperature (Tg) of the hydrogel shifted from 108.8 °C to 101.5 °C, indicating enhanced elasticity and chain mobility for dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel concurrently treated at pH 6.8 and 0.1 mM H2O2 [44]. Additionally, the TGA results showed a shift of degradation temperature to a lower temperature in the range of 300–600 °C, correlated with the network of the hydrogel (Fig. 2 e).

The dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was subjected to compression force, and its mechanical performance was recorded and calculated. Fig. 3
a revealed a higher compression stress (4.3 kPa) at the 40 % strain of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel at 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8 than that at pH 7.4 (2.7 kPa), pH 6.8 (3.1 kPa), and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 7.4 (2.8 kPa). Moreover, the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel exhibited the compression modulus of 46.9 Pa and 45.5 Pa at pH 7.4 and 6.8, respectively, whereas it was 47.8 Pa and 105.0 Pa in the presence of H2O2 at 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 7.4 and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8, respectively (Fig. 3 b). Similarly, dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel exhibited a compression toughness of 5.9 J/m2 (pH 7.4) and 5.7 J/m2 (pH 6.8) under pH conditions without H2O2. For simultaneous pH and H2O2 treatment, dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel exhibited a compression toughness of 7.6 J/m2 and 16.2 J/m2 at 0.1 mM H2O2/ pH 7.4 and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8, respectively. The higher compression modulus under dual-stimuli conditions, such as pH and H2O2, was due to increased hydrogen bonding after exposure to both acidic pH and higher H2O2 concentration. The cleavage of boronate ester bonds to B-OH and diselenide bonds to Se-OOH is crucial for controlling and improving the mechanical properties of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel.

The resistance of the hydrogel was measured using a Keithley 2450 Sourcemeter, as depicted in Fig. 3 c. The electrical conductivity of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel at physiological pH (pH 7.4) served as a control, showing a resistance (R) of 78.9 kΩ. When the environmental pH was adjusted to 6.8, imitating the extracellular pH of cancer, the R-value was reduced to 54.0 kΩ. Moreover, a decrease to 45.5 kΩ at 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 7.4 was observed, and it was further reduced to 38.8 kΩ at 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8. The alterations in electrical conductivity was influenced by changes in the size of the loaded particles within the hydrogel matrix, impacting electron mobility and the probability of contact when subjected to an electrical current [45]. The Nyquist plot (EIS) (Fig. 3 d) mirrored the resistance findings of Sourcemeter: displaying a high impedance at pH 7.4 and the lowest resistance at 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8. This observation was further corroborated by wireless analysis (Fig. 3 e), where higher resistance corresponded to a higher line position; resistance decreased with both pH and H2O2 treatment (lower line position). Finally, the electronic resistance signals of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel may be affected by differing tissue environment in the body hence, the resistance of the sensor was analyzed in the presence of various interfering agents like fetal bovine serum (FBS), horse serum, glucose and ascorbic acid and their effect when combined with various pH and 0.1 mM H2O2 (Fig. S8). The sensor showed minimal lowering of resistance at pH 7.4 in presence of FBS, horse serum owing to the presence of electrolytes and in case of ascorbic acid caused by the presence of ions. The resistance increased for glucose owing to the presence of vicinal diol groups which reacts with the boronic acid and catechol groups in the dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles. Moreover, the resistance of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel decreased at pH 6.8 with further decrease in resistance observed with the introduction of 0.1 mM H2O2 in the mixed solution demonstrating the specificity of the sensor towards acidic pH and 0.1 mM H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The H2O2- and pH-responsive electrical signal of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel by mechanical deformation
ce:section.ce:para:
As the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel presented mechanical adjustment and distinct resistance owing to the effect of pH and H2O2, the electrical signal of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel after deformation was investigated. The initial assessment involved the adhesiveness and stability of the hydrogel under three types of mechanical deformations (bending 1, 2, and 3) as depicted in Fig. 4
a and S9. The evaluations were performed under two separate conditions: open-air and underwater. The dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel demonstrated excellent adhesiveness in both circumstances during bending, and notably, the attachment of hydrogel remained stable underwater. Moreover, the electro-mechanical signal of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was quantified by determining the relative resistance change (ΔR/R0). The ΔR/R0 of the hydrogel at pH 7.4, pH 6.8, 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 7.4, and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8 were measured as 17.6 %, 19.1 %, 37.5 %, and 46.2 %, respectively (Fig. 4 b). The increased response after pH and H2O2 treatment was attributed to the adjustment in mechanical properties caused by network rearrangement involving the cleavage of pH-responsive boronate ester bonds and H2O2-sensitive diselenide bonds. Additionally, the stability of the electrochemical sensing signals of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was assessed by analyzing the recyclability of the bending response for 50 cycles as given in Fig. S10. It was seen that the sensor displayed distinct repetitive signals at both pH 7.4 and 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8 highlighting its recyclability.

The electrical signals in response to bending at different angles were measured using Wayne 4100 LCR meter (Fig. 4 c). The ΔR/R0 of pH 7.4-treated dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was calculated as 3.6 %, 7.0 %, 9.9 %, and 12.9 % for 30°, 60°, 90°, and 120°, respectively. Contrastingly, the responses were elevated to higher responses of 12.9 %, 19.2 %, 28.0 %, and 33.7 % at bending angles of 30°, 60°, 90°, and 120°, respectively, for the hydrogel treated with 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8. This increment in resistance with an increase in the degree of bending correlated with the enlargement in the dimensional length of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel during deformation [46]. The results of the wireless analysis further supported the outcomes of relative resistance change (Fig. 4 d). The mechanical–electrical correlation was further investigated by repeatedly applying 30 % strain to dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel. The dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel exhibited the most significant relative resistance change at 0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8 compared to other conditions. This observation provides additional clarity on the impact of concurrent pH and H2O2 treatment on the hydrogel performance (Fig. S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro study of cancer cell selective electrical signal using dsCD-PD(HA) hydrogel sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, time-dependent in vitro cancer detection based on bending mechanical deformation and electrical resistance was performed (Fig. 5 c). For CHO-K1 treated for 24 h, the average ΔR/R0 was 4.3 %, 8.9 %, 13.4 %, and 17.6 % at bending angles of 30°, 60°, 90°, and 120°, respectively. In terms of the bending angle, the HeLa-embedded dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel exhibited elevated signals of 7.3 %, 14.1 %, 18.7 %, and 23.2 % after 24 h of treatment, mirroring the responses observed with PC-3 cells (6.5 %, 11.9 %, 17.5 %, and 21.3 %, respectively). The CHO-K1-embedded hydrogel showed insignificant increase in ΔR/R0 with increase in treatment time to 48 h, compared to HeLa and PC-3, in which ΔR/R0 elevated to 26.2 % and 25.9 % at a bending angle of 120°. The change in electrical response correspond with molecular adjustments, implicating the breakdown of boronate ester and diselenide bonds at low cellular pH and higher ROS concentrations in cancer cell lines. Assessment of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel adhesion after in vitro treatment were conducted across different surface types, as given in Fig. S19. The hydrogel displayed notable adherence after treatment with CHO-K1, HeLa, and PC-3 cells, demonstrating attachment to glass, steel, PET, and rubber substrates. Furthermore, the recyclability of the in vitro bending signals were conducted for 50 cycles to assess the stability of the electrical signals of the sensor. It can be seen from Fig. S20 that the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel sensor displayed reusability based on its stable bending response signals in presence of CHO-K1 as the normal cells and HeLa and PC-3 as the cancer cells.

Following verification of the in vitro electrochemical sensing performance of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel, the cytotoxicity of the sensor was observed in the presence of cells. The Live and Dead cell viability assay in the presence of CHO-K1, HeLa, and PC-3 cells showed that most cells survived (green fluorescence), with a negligible number of dead cells (red fluorescence) ( Fig. 6
a). Subsequently, the effects of intracellular ROS and pH on the in vitro optical performance of the sensor were analyzed. The time-dependent cellular uptake of loaded nanoparticles by CHO-K1, HeLa, and PC-3 cells post-treatment with the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel for 1.5, 3, and 6 h was evaluated, as depicted in Fig. 6 b. Green fluorescence started to appear within 1.5 h of treatment, which increased until 6 h of treatment, demonstrating successful cellular uptake of nanoparticles and indicating the cleavage of dsCD-PD(HA) nanoparticles in the hydrogel in the presence of a higher amount of ROS in cancer cells. The ROS scavenging performance of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel was also analyzed. The cells were embedded in the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel, and ROS concentration in the cells was observed over time (1.5, 3, 6, and 12 h) after staining with dihydroethidium (DHE) [57]. High levels of DHE-specific red fluorescence was detected in the cancer cells HeLa and PC-3, whereas minimal fluorescence was observed in CHO-K1 (normal cells) at 0 h (Fig. 6 c). This difference in intensity was correlated with the ROS concentration in the cells. The red fluorescence diminished in all cells following treatment with dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel, suggesting the ability of the hydrogel to reduce and scavenge ROS within the cells. Additionally, the confocal image of the dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel surface fluorescence was observed after embedding, depicting an increase in the green fluorescence intensity for PC-3 and HeLa cells after 12 h. In contrast, low fluorescence was observed for CHO-K1 cells even after 48 h, suggesting sensitivity of the hydrogel towards cancer microenvironments (Fig. S21). Finally, the detection of the cancer microenvironment based on the optical, electrical, and mechanical modifications of the hydrogel sensor showed promising results, particularly through pH and ROS levels in cancer cells.

In this study, cancer cell selective changed electrical signal sensor using diselenide-functionalized carbon dot-integrated hyaluronic acid polymer dots (dsCD-PD(HA))-loaded conductive (dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel) capable of detecting cellular ROS (e.g., H2O2) and pH, was developed. The fabricated hydrogel effectively distinguished cancer cells from normal cells in response to cellular factors, as evidenced by the higher fluorescence intensity observed for dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel at acidic pH and higher H2O2 concentration. The acidic cancer microenvironment prompted the recovery of fluorescence of dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel via cleaving the boronate ester bond within dsCD-PD(HA), which had previously been quenched by the FRET effect. Moreover, the hydrogel sensor demonstrated a shift in electrical conductivity from 78.9 kΩ (pH 7.4) to 38.8 kΩ (0.1 mM H2O2/pH 6.8) due to the cleavage of the diselenide bonds of dsCD. The correlation between in vitro bending-electrical response correlation was simulated, revealing electronic and mechanical modifications indicated by differences in ΔR/R0 profiles between cancer (26.2 % and 25.9 % for HeLa and PC-3, respectively) and normal cells (17.9 %, CHO-K1). Additionally, in vitro optical analysis highlighted the effectiveness of the sensor in distinguishing cancer (HeLa and PC-3) cells from normal (CHO-K1) cells via elevated fluorescence recovery in the case of cancer cells, along with its favorable biocompatibility and in vitro ROS scavenging ability. Finally, a wireless sensing system was integrated with a dsCD-PD(HA) Hydrogel sensor for precise smartphone monitoring. Thus, this hydrogel can be used as a reliable electrochemical biosensor to detect cancer microenvironments.

Hayeon Jo: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Benny Ryplida: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Kaustuv Roy: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sung Young Park: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean Government (MSIT) (No. RS-202300207925, and 2018R1A6A1A03023788) and by “Regional Innovation Strategy (RIS)” through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (MOE)(2021RIS-001).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2024.06.027.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

Z. An, L. Zhang, Y. Liu, H. Zhao, Y. Zhang, Y. Cao, Y. Zhang, R. Pei, Biomater. Sci. 10(1) (2022) 100–13. 10.1039/D1BM01179K.
